FullText: The birth genesis : origin beginning of Jesus Christ was as follows : His mother Mary had been promised to Joseph in marriage . But before they were married , Mary realized that she was pregnant by Holy Spirit .

DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had been promised' describes a change of social/legal status (betrothal/engagement), and that status is currently held at the reference time, as indicated by the subsequent clause 'before they were married' which presupposes the promise remains in effect.

FullText: When Joseph woke up , he did what the angel of God had commanded him to do . He took Mary to be his wife .

DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had commanded' describes a change of social/legal status (issuing a binding directive) where the command remains in effect at the reference time, as evidenced by Joseph's subsequent compliance with what was commanded.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'had commanded' denotes a discrete bounded event (the angel's command) that occurred prior to the reference time, not a continuous situation holding throughout a time span.

FullText: » Do not pray like the hypocrites ! They want everyone to see them in the houses of worship and on the street corners . I assure you they have been paid in full .

TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'they have been paid in full' presents a passive bounded transition event (payment as a completed whole) with a completion-measure adverbial ('in full') that profiles the situation as bounded, matching the set_to_1_if criteria.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have been paid' is asserted as a realized fact in the actual world within the speaker's assurance ('I assure you'), with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical context present.

TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'They have received their reward' presents a bounded completion event (the transition of receiving) as a single accomplished whole, functioning as a discrete narrative statement that asserts a completed transition.

FullText: When Jesus had finished these words , the crowds were astonished at what he taught .

AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had finished' denotes a bounded event with an inherent endpoint (completion of speaking the words) and entails a defined result state, satisfying the set_to_0_if criteria.

DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'had finished' denotes a punctual transition into a completed state (finishing the words) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and there is no duration adverbial indicating extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'had finished' is a transition event (completion of an action) construed as a bounded whole, and the clause functions as a discrete step in narrative sequence ('When...then...'), packaging the situation as a completed occurrence.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had finished' is in a temporal clause ('When Jesus had finished...') that asserts a realized event in the narrative sequence; it is not modal, conditional, counterfactual, or future-marked, but rather establishes a completed fact that precedes the main clause event.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, forming a past perfect construction ('had finished'), which instantiates TLTS according to the first set_to_1_if criterion.

FullText: When Jesus heard it , he was amazed , and said to the crowd : » I tell you , I have not found such great faith in Israel .

TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have not found' is a present perfect assertion of a realized fact in the actual world (Jesus's experience of not finding such faith), with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical marking.

FullText: They screamed at him , saying : » What have we to do with you , Son of God ? Have you come to torment us before the time ? «

TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'Have you come to torment us before the time?' presents 'come' as a transition event (arrival/appearance) construed as a completed whole, with the infinitive 'to torment' indicating purposive aspect, and the temporal frame 'before the time' establishes a bounded endpoint.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'Have you come' appears in an interrogative clause expressing a hypothetical or questioning evaluation of purpose ('to torment us before the time'), presenting the situation as uncertain or projected rather than asserted as realized fact.

FullText: But when the people saw it , they marveled , and glorified God who had given such authority to men .

FullText: » Why patch an old garment with cloth that has not been shrunk ? The patch shrinks and pulls away from the old cloth leaving a big hole .

FullText: Jesus turned to see her and said : » Daughter , take courage ; your faith has made you well . « The woman was healed at that time .

VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'your faith' is the semantic agent (doer) and 'you' is the semantic patient (object), with active voice morphology 'has made' rather than passive auxiliary 'has been made'.

FullText: When the demon was cast out , the dumb man spoke . The crowd marveled , saying : » We have never seen anything like this in Israel ! «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'seen' introduces an overt grammatical subject 'We' and an overt direct object 'anything', satisfying the first set_to_1 condition.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'seen' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'we') and takes a direct object ('anything like this'), satisfying the set_to_0_if condition for clauses containing overt objects introduced by the target verb.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'seen' licenses an internal argument ('anything like this'), making it transitive and thus not an unergative one-participant event.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'seen' denotes a punctual perceptual event without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint; it cannot be naturally modified by 'in X time' to indicate completion of a bounded process, and the negation 'never' emphasizes the absence of the punctual event rather than an incomplete process.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'seen' denotes a punctual perceptual transition into awareness or recognition (perceiving something for the first time), which is a near-instantaneous crossing of a threshold from not-knowing to knowing, satisfying the AktAch feature criteria.
AktAct_explanation: The verb phrase 'have never seen' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness (seeing something for the first time), which is a single completed occurrence rather than a dynamic durative situation.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have never seen' entails a resulting state of non-experience over an extended time period rather than a single punctual occurrence, and the perfect aspect construes it as a durative situation extending from past to present.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have never seen' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness (the moment of perceiving something), which encodes eventive change rather than a static condition, triggering set_to_0_if criterion 4.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'We have never seen anything like this in Israel' presents a factual statement about the crowd's actual experience (they genuinely have not witnessed such an event), not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The adverb 'never' directly modifies the target gram 'have seen', negating the eventuality of seeing, which matches the set_to_1_if criterion.
DtExp_explanation: The gram is a negative Perfect ('have never seen') which functions as a claim about the absence of the event in the subject's life history, matching the set_to_1_if criterion for negative Perfects.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'see' is a perception event that does not entail a resulting state persisting at the reference time; the clause expresses a negative experiential claim (never witnessed) rather than a transition to a state currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (never witnessing something like this) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and lacks duration adverbials indicating extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'We have never seen anything like this' is a stative situation (the predicate 'see' in the sense of 'experience/witness') without a construed boundary; the negation with 'never' and the lack of a completion-measure or transition construal indicate an unbounded, open situation rather than a bounded whole.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have never seen' is a present perfect assertion of a realized fact in the actual world (the crowd's actual experience), not a modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical construction.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have never seen' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle ('seen') with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have never seen' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'We' as the semantic agent (doer) and 'anything' as the grammatical object (semantic patient), lacking the passive morphology of 'have + been + past participle'.

FullText: » When they persecute you in this city , flee to the next . I tell you ; before you have gone through all the cities of Israel the Son of man will come .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have gone through' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('you') and an overt direct object ('all the cities of Israel'), satisfying the transitive pattern required for ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'go through' licenses an external argument (the subject 'you') that functions as an agent/initiator performing the action of traversing the cities, and the clause contains a direct object ('all the cities of Israel'), making it transitive rather than unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'have gone through' licenses an internal argument ('all the cities of Israel'), making it transitive and thus not an unergative one-participant event.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'have gone through all the cities of Israel' involves directed motion toward a defined spatial goal (completing passage through all cities), which is a durative process culminating in a terminal point, satisfying the AktAcc feature.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have gone through' denotes a durative process of traversing multiple cities over measurable time, not a punctual transition into a new state; it can be naturally modified by 'finish' ('finish going through all the cities'), indicating a completion of an unfolding process phase rather than a boundary-crossing event.
AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'have gone through all the cities' entails a specific, inherent endpoint (completing passage through all cities) and can be modified by 'in X time' (e.g., 'before you have gone through all the cities in three days'), indicating a bounded, telic event rather than a durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have gone through all the cities' entails a transition into a resulting state (completion of traversing cities) and unfolds over measurable duration rather than occurring as a single punctual occurrence.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have gone through' denotes a dynamic change-of-state event involving progression through a sequence of cities; it involves internal eventive unfolding and bounded completion, not a static condition.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'before you have gone through all the cities of Israel' presents a future temporal condition within an open conditional context ('I tell you'), not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have gone through' is not negated; the negation ('before') is a temporal conjunction indicating the event will occur prior to another event, not a negator of the target gram itself.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have gone through' entails a change of location/completion of a bounded process (traversing all cities), and the clause projects this completion as a condition for a future event, treating the resulting state of having completed the traversal as relevant to the reference time.
DtRes_explanation: The motion verb 'gone through' describes a journey through cities, but the context indicates a future hypothetical scenario ('before you have gone through...the Son of man will come'), not a completed transition with a persisting resulting state at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (completing passage through all cities) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and the focus is on the transition/completion of that bounded action.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'have gone through' construes 'going through all the cities' as a bounded transition event with a construed endpoint, functioning as a discrete temporal milestone in the narrative sequence ('before you have gone through...the Son of man will come').
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have gone through' appears in a temporal clause ('before you have gone through all the cities') that is part of a future prediction ('the Son of man will come'), presenting the situation as projected and not yet realized.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have gone through' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle 'gone through', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'you' is the semantic agent (doer) performing the action of going through cities, and 'all the cities of Israel' is the semantic patient (object affected), making this active voice, not passive voice.

FullText: » From the days of John the Baptist until now , the kingdom of heaven has been man’s goal , and men of violence have attempted to take it by force .
ASTrns_explanation: The passive construction 'has been [man's goal]' licenses both an external argument (the kingdom of heaven as the affected entity) and an internal argument (man's goal as the predicative complement), meeting the passive criterion for ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: This is a copular clause with 'be' where the sole argument 'the kingdom of heaven' functions as the syntactic subject linked to the predicative complement 'man's goal', and no external argument is licensed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The target 'has been' is a copular verb that licenses an internal argument (the predicate nominal 'man's goal'), not a one-participant unergative verb with only an external argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The target 'has been' denotes a stative condition (the kingdom being a goal) without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint; it describes a persistent state rather than an event that culminates in a result state.
AktAch_explanation: The verb phrase 'has been' denotes a stative condition of existing as a goal over an extended duration ('from the days of John the Baptist until now'), not a punctual transition into a new state, and lacks the instantaneous boundary-crossing characteristic of AktAch.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'has been' with the complement 'man's goal' denotes a stative situation lacking dynamic unfolding and sustained engagement; it expresses a defined result state (the kingdom being established as a goal) rather than an ongoing activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'has been' expresses a state of being that unfolds over an extended duration (from John the Baptist until now) rather than a single punctual dynamic occurrence, and it does not describe a dynamic event but rather a stative condition.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'has been' is morphologically marked as progressive perfect aspect, which entails dynamic unfolding and is explicitly incompatible with stative readings per the set_to_0_if criteria.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents a situation that has actually occurred in the actual world (the kingdom of heaven has been a goal from John the Baptist's time until now), without any indication of counterfactuality, contrary-to-fact conditions, or unfulfilled wishes.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has been' is not negated; it affirms that the kingdom of heaven has been man's goal, and no negator (not, n't, never, or negative quantifier) directly negates this eventuality.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has been' entails a change of state (the kingdom becoming man's goal) that persists at the reference time, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for resulting states that hold at reference time.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has been' with the predicate 'man's goal' describes a continuous state rather than a change of state; the duration adverbial 'from the days of John the Baptist until now' indicates an ongoing situation without a transition to a resulting state, triggering the set_to_0 condition.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause contains the duration adverbial 'From the days of John the Baptist until now' indicating a situation (the kingdom of heaven being man's goal) that extends continuously from a past starting point to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'has been man's goal' presents a stative situation (being a goal) without a construed boundary or transition; it describes an ongoing state from the past until now rather than a bounded completed event.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has been' asserts the kingdom of heaven as man's goal as a realized fact from the past until now, with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical marking.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has been' includes 'been' followed by a progressive participle context (the kingdom of heaven has been man's goal), which matches the set_to_1_if criterion for progressive perfect forms.
VcPass_explanation: The complement of 'been' is the noun phrase 'man's goal' (a predicate nominative), making 'been' a copular verb rather than a passive auxiliary, so VcPass is not instantiated.

FullText: » From the days of John the Baptist until now , the kingdom of heaven has been man’s goal , and men of violence have attempted to take it by force .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have attempted' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('men of violence') and an overt direct object ('to take it by force'), satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition for a transitive construction with both external and internal arguments.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'attempted' licenses an external argument (the agent 'men of violence') and introduces a direct object ('to take it'), satisfying the set_to_0_if criteria for verbs that license external arguments and contain overt objects.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'attempted' licenses an internal argument (the infinitival complement 'to take it by force'), which means it does not instantiate ASUnrg, which requires no internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'have attempted to take it by force' denotes a durative process directed toward a defined goal (taking the kingdom by force), which naturally fits the 'in X time' test and involves directed action toward an abstract goal that defines completion.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have attempted' denotes an ongoing or repeated effort to achieve a goal rather than a punctual transition into a new state; 'attempt' unfolds as a durative process phase and can naturally be modified by 'finish' (e.g., 'finished attempting'), which indicates a process rather than an instantaneous achievement.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have attempted to take it by force' denotes a bounded event with an inherent endpoint (taking the kingdom by force), not a dynamic durative activity; it entails a specific goal-directed result state rather than homogeneous ongoing action.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have attempted to take it by force' entails a transition into a resulting state (the attempt itself constitutes a change from not-attempting to attempting) and unfolds over measurable duration rather than occurring as a single punctual occurrence.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have attempted' denotes a dynamic activity involving volitional engagement and eventive unfolding (the act of trying to take the kingdom by force), which lacks the static, unchanging quality required for AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents 'have attempted' as a situation that has actually occurred in the actual world (men of violence have indeed attempted to take the kingdom by force), with no indication of counterfactuality, contrary-to-fact condition, or unfulfilled wish.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have attempted' is not negated; the negation does not apply to this verb phrase but rather to the embedded infinitive 'to take it by force' (men have attempted NOT to take it, but rather have attempted to take it).
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have attempted to take it by force' entails a change of state or action with a resulting effect (taking the kingdom by force), and the clause presents this as a persisting historical reality rather than merely recording whether the experience occurred in the subject's life history.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'attempt' describes an activity or effort without entailing a resulting state that persists at the reference time; the clause does not indicate that the men currently hold the kingdom or that a change of state is in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'men of violence have attempted to take it by force' describes a repeated or habitual series of events ('men of violence' as a group making multiple attempts) that characterizes the entire time span from 'the days of John the Baptist until now', matching the iterative series criterion for DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'have attempted' construes 'attempt' as a transition event (a discrete action with a bounded endpoint) asserted as completed, and the clause functions as a discrete step in the narrative sequence describing historical actions of men of violence.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have attempted' is in present perfect tense asserting a realized fact about men of violence in the actual world, with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical markers present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have attempted' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the simple present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have attempted' is active voice with 'men of violence' as the grammatical subject (semantic agent) and 'it' as the grammatical object (semantic patient), not passive voice.

FullText: » Woe to you , Chorazin ! Woe to you , Bethsaida ! If the mighty works had been done in Tyre and Sidon that were done in you , they would have repented long ago in sackcloth and ashes .
ASTrns_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('had been done'), and the lexical verb 'do' licenses both an external argument (agent, unexpressed) and an internal argument (patient, 'the mighty works'), satisfying the passive condition for ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('had been done'), and the lexical verb 'do' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent performing the mighty works), which triggers the set_to_0_if condition for passive clauses.
ASUnrg_explanation: The target verb 'had been done' is a passive construction where the grammatical subject 'mighty works' functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) undergoing the action rather than as an external argument (agent/initiator), thus failing the ASUnrg criterion.
AktAcc_explanation: The passive construction 'had been done' denotes a punctual or near-instantaneous completion of an action (the performance of mighty works) without a durative process phase, and the counterfactual conditional context emphasizes the boundary event rather than a gradual culmination toward a goal.
AktAch_explanation: The passive construction 'had been done' denotes a completed durative process (performing mighty works) rather than a punctual transition into a new state, and naturally permits the complement of 'finish' (e.g., 'if the mighty works had been finished being done'), indicating an unfolding process phase rather than a boundary-crossing event.
AktAct_explanation: The passive construction 'had been done' denotes a bounded, completed event with an inherent endpoint (the accomplishment of the mighty works), not a dynamic durative activity without inherent completion.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had been done' entails a resulting change of state (the completion and result of performing mighty works), and the past perfect construction indicates a durative or completed process rather than a single punctual occurrence.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'had been done' is morphologically marked as progressive perfect aspect (past perfect progressive), which entails dynamic eventive unfolding and is explicitly incompatible with stative readings per the set_to_0_if criteria.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'had been done' occurs in the protasis of a counterfactual conditional ('If the mighty works had been done in Tyre and Sidon...') paired with the apodosis 'they would have repented', expressing a situation contrary to fact—the mighty works were not done in those cities, so the repentance did not occur.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had been done' appears in a counterfactual conditional clause ('If the mighty works had been done...') where negation applies to the entire hypothetical scenario rather than directly negating the auxiliary with 'not'/'n't', a negative quantifier, or 'never'.
DtExp_explanation: The clause 'had been done in Tyre and Sidon' presents a counterfactual conditional where the focus is on a hypothetical change of state (the performance of mighty works in those cities), not on whether the event occurred in the subject's life history; this is a result state entailment in a conditional structure, not a life-history entry.
DtRes_explanation: The clause is counterfactual ('If the mighty works had been done...') negating the actual occurrence of the event, so no transition to a resulting state is entailed at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a hypothetical discrete event (the performance of mighty works in Tyre and Sidon) rather than a situation holding continuously over time; it is a counterfactual conditional focused on a bounded occurrence, not duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'had been done in Tyre and Sidon' is a counterfactual conditional (if-clause) that presents a hypothetical, unrealized situation rather than a bounded completed event, and the negation of the actual occurrence keeps the situation open rather than asserting it as a bounded whole.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had been done' appears in a counterfactual conditional construction ('If the mighty works had been done...they would have repented'), presenting a hypothetical, non-actual situation rather than a realized fact.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'had been done' contains the past-tense auxiliary 'had' followed by 'been' and a past participle, instantiating both past perfect and progressive perfect marking, which satisfies the set_to_1_if conditions.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had been done' instantiates VcPass: it has the form auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle of transitive verb 'done', the grammatical subject 'mighty works' is the semantic patient (entity affected by the action), and the agent is omitted, satisfying passive voice morphology.

FullText: » Woe to you , Chorazin ! Woe to you , Bethsaida ! If the mighty works had been done in Tyre and Sidon that were done in you , they would have repented long ago in sackcloth and ashes .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'repented' does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); it is used intransitively in an activity reading where no affected patient is syntactically realized.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'repent' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'they' who perform the action of repenting), making it unergative/transitive rather than unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'repented' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('they') functioning as an external argument (agent engaged in a volitional activity) with no internal argument licensed or realized.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'repent' denotes a gradual change-of-state or internal transformation culminating in a defined result condition (a state of repentance), and the adverbial 'long ago' combined with the conditional structure indicates a bounded, durative process directed toward a defined endpoint.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'repented' denotes a punctual transition into a new state of mind/spiritual condition, can be modified by the time-point adverbial 'long ago', and represents a definable threshold crossing rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'repent' denotes a bounded change of state with an inherent endpoint (transition from unrepentant to repentant), not a dynamic durative activity that unfolds without inherent completion.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'repented' entails a transition into a new resulting state (a change from unrepentant to repentant condition), which disqualifies it from AktSemel status.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'would have repented' denotes a bounded change-of-state event (transition from unrepentant to repentant condition) involving dynamic progression, which is incompatible with AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'would have repented' occurs in the apodosis (result clause) of a counterfactual conditional introduced by 'If the mighty works had been done in Tyre and Sidon...', expressing an unfulfilled past event contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'would have repented' is not negated; it appears in a counterfactual conditional expressing what would have happened under different circumstances, but the eventuality itself is not presented as absent or not occurring.
DtExp_explanation: The counterfactual conditional 'would have repented' presents repentance as a bounded activity/experience that would have occurred at least once in response to the hypothetical condition, focusing on the occurrence of the event rather than a persisting result state.
DtRes_explanation: The target is a counterfactual conditional ('would have repented') expressing what would have happened under unmet conditions, not a change of state that holds at the reference time; the resulting state does not currently obtain.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'would have repented' describes a discrete bounded event (a single act of repentance) rather than a continuous state or habitual series extending to the reference time, and the counterfactual conditional structure focuses on the transition into the state of having repented rather than duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'would have repented' construes 'repent' (a transition event) as a single bounded, completed occurrence in a counterfactual conditional, presenting the situation as a whole rather than as ongoing or habitual.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'would have repented' appears in a counterfactual conditional construction ('If the mighty works had been done...they would have repented'), presenting a hypothetical situation contrary to fact rather than asserting a realized event.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'would have repented' includes the modal auxiliary 'would' preceding 'have', which matches the set_to_1_if criterion for modal perfect forms.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'would have repented' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'they' as the semantic agent (the ones doing the repenting), not a passive construction with auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle.

FullText: » Will you , Capernaum , be exalted into heaven ? You will go down into the grave . If the mighty works had been done in Sodom , which were done in you , it would have remained until this day .
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'had been done' is a passive construction that licenses both an external argument (agent, unexpressed) and an internal argument (patient, 'the mighty works'), satisfying the passive condition in set_to_1_if.
ASUncc_explanation: The target 'had been done' is a passive construction where the lexical verb 'do' licenses an external argument (the agent performing the mighty works), which is suppressed in the passive voice, triggering the set_to_0_if condition for passive clauses.
ASUnrg_explanation: The target verb 'had been done' is a passive construction that licenses an internal argument (the implied theme/patient 'mighty works'), making it transitive and thus not an unergative one-participant event with an external argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The passive construction 'had been done' denotes a punctual or near-instantaneous completion of an action (the performance of mighty works) without emphasizing a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint; it marks a boundary transition rather than a gradual, process-oriented change of state.
AktAch_explanation: The passive construction 'had been done' denotes a completed durative process of performing mighty works rather than a punctual transition into a new state, and naturally permits the complement of 'finish' (e.g., 'if the mighty works had been finished being done'), indicating an unfolding process phase rather than a boundary-crossing event.
AktAct_explanation: The passive construction 'had been done' denotes a completed action with a defined result state (the mighty works were performed and finished), satisfying the set_to_0_if criterion of entailing a specific, inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had been done' entails a resulting change of state (the completion and establishment of mighty works in Sodom), and the past perfect construction indicates a durative or completed process rather than a single punctual occurrence.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'had been done' is morphologically marked as progressive perfect aspect (past perfect progressive), which entails dynamic eventive unfolding and is explicitly incompatible with stative readings per the set_to_0_if criteria.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'had been done' occurs in the protasis of a counterfactual conditional ('If the mighty works had been done in Sodom...it would have remained'), where the condition is understood not to have been satisfied in the actual world.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had been done' appears in a conditional clause ('If the mighty works had been done in Sodom') where no negator directly negates the auxiliary or the eventuality itself; the negation is implicit in the counterfactual conditional structure but does not syntactically negate the target gram.
DtExp_explanation: The clause 'had been done in Sodom' is a counterfactual conditional expressing a hypothetical change of state (the performance of mighty works), and the consequent 'it would have remained' indicates a resulting state that would persist, triggering the set_to_0 condition for verbs entailing change of state.
DtRes_explanation: The clause is counterfactual ('If the mighty works had been done...') negating the actual occurrence of the event in Sodom, so no resulting state transition is entailed at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a hypothetical discrete event (the performance of mighty works in Sodom) rather than a situation holding continuously over time; the focus is on the counterfactual occurrence of bounded actions, not on duration or continuous habitual activity.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'had been done' is a passive form asserting a bounded transition event (the completion of mighty works) as a single whole within a counterfactual conditional, presenting the situation as a completed occurrence rather than an ongoing process.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had been done' appears in a counterfactual conditional construction ('If the mighty works had been done in Sodom...it would have remained'), presenting a hypothetical, non-actual situation rather than a realized fact.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'had been done' contains the morphologically past auxiliary 'had' followed by 'been' and a past participle, instantiating both past perfect and progressive perfect features, which triggers TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had been done' instantiates VcPass: it has the form auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle of transitive verb 'done', the grammatical subject 'mighty works' is the semantic patient (entity affected), and the passive voice is overtly marked.

FullText: » Will you , Capernaum , be exalted into heaven ? You will go down into the grave . If the mighty works had been done in Sodom , which were done in you , it would have remained until this day .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'remained' is intransitive and does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); it only takes an external argument (subject) and functions as a stative verb without affecting a patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'remained' denotes a state of existence/persistence where the sole argument (Sodom, implied) functions as an internal argument (theme), and the verb does not license an external argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'remained' denotes a change-of-state or stative condition where the sole argument (Sodom) functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) undergoing or experiencing a state, not as an external argument initiating an action.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'would have remained' denotes a stative condition without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint; it describes a continuous state persisting until a temporal boundary, not a dynamic event culminating in a result state.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'remained' denotes a durative state of persistence over a measurable duration ('until this day'), not a punctual transition into a new state, and fails all set_to_1 criteria.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'would have remained' denotes a bounded state with a specific endpoint ('until this day'), indicating a defined temporal limit rather than a dynamic, durative activity without inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'would have remained' entails a transition into and continuation of a resulting state (Sodom standing until that day), which violates the requirement that AktSemel events must not entail a resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The conditional perfect 'would have remained' denotes a durative state of persistence over time without dynamic change or internal eventive unfolding, meeting the criteria for AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'would have remained' occurs in the apodosis of a counterfactual conditional ('If the mighty works had been done in Sodom...it would have remained'), expressing a result that did not actually occur.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'would have remained' is not negated; the negation in the conditional clause ('If the mighty works had been done') applies to the antecedent condition, not to the eventuality of remaining.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'remained' entails a change of state (Sodom would have persisted in an exalted condition), and the duration adverbial 'until this day' indicates a continuing situation rather than a completed life experience, triggering the set_to_0 criteria.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'remain' describes a durative state rather than a change of state, and the counterfactual conditional structure ('if...would have') negates the actual occurrence of the event, so no resulting state transition is entailed at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause contains the duration adverbial 'until this day' indicating a situation (Sodom's standing/existence) that would have extended continuously from a past point to the reference time, matching the DtUniv feature.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'would have remained' denotes a stative situation (remaining in existence) without a construed boundary or transition; the conditional counterfactual context and duration adverbial 'until this day' profile the internal temporal span rather than packaging it as a bounded whole.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'would have remained' appears in a counterfactual conditional construction ('If the mighty works had been done in Sodom...it would have remained'), presenting a hypothetical situation contrary to fact rather than asserting a realized event.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'would have remained' includes the modal auxiliary 'would' preceding 'have', which instantiates the TLTS feature according to the set_to_1_if rule for modal perfect forms.

FullText: Then Jesus prayed : » I thank you , O Father , Lord of heaven and earth . You have hidden these things from the wise and understanding , and have revealed them to children .

ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'have hidden' licenses an external argument (You/God as agent) and introduces a direct object ('these things'), satisfying the set_to_0_if criteria for agentive verbs with overt objects.

AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have hidden' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (concealment from perception) and can be modified by a specific time-point adverbial ('You have hidden these things at that moment'), meeting the criteria for AktAch.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have hidden' denotes a completed action with a defined result state (things are now hidden as a consequence), lacks dynamic durative unfolding, and cannot naturally be modified by 'for X time' without changing meaning, making it a perfective accomplishment rather than an activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have hidden' entails a resulting change of state (things transitioning from visible/known to hidden/concealed), which disqualifies it from AktSemel status.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have hidden' denotes a change-of-state event (concealment) involving dynamic progression from a state of visibility to hiddenness, which is incompatible with the stative feature AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'You have hidden these things' presents an action understood to have actually occurred in the actual world (a past action with present relevance in Jesus's prayer), without any indication that it is contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have hidden' is not negated; it is a positive assertion in a parallel structure with 'have revealed', and no negator applies to it.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have hidden' entails a change of state (things being in a hidden condition) that persists at the reference time, making it describe a resulting state rather than a life-history experience.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have hidden' describes a change of state (concealment) where things are currently in a hidden state at the reference time, and the parallel structure with 'have revealed' reinforces that both resulting states (hidden and revealed) persist.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'have hidden' denotes a completed action with a resultant state (things are now hidden from the wise), focusing on the transition/result rather than a situation holding continuously over a time span, and lacks duration adverbials or habitual markers required for DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'have hidden' is a transition event (hide = to cause something to become hidden) construed as a completed whole in a narrative/discourse context, presenting a bounded occurrence rather than an ongoing or stative situation.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have hidden' is asserted as a realized fact in the actual world within a statement of gratitude, with no modal auxiliary, conditional, counterfactual, or future marking present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have hidden' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the simple present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'You' is the semantic agent (doer) performing the action of hiding, and 'these things' is the grammatical object serving as the semantic patient; this is active voice, not passive voice with auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle.

AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have revealed' entails a resulting change of state (things transition from hidden to revealed), which disqualifies it from AktSemel status.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have revealed' denotes a dynamic change-of-state event (making something known that was previously hidden), involving internal eventive unfolding and a clear boundary between the state before and after revelation, which is incompatible with AktState.

ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have revealed' is not negated; it appears in a positive coordinate clause parallel to 'have hidden' and expresses an event that is realized rather than absent.

TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have revealed' is a present perfect form asserting a realized action in the actual world within Jesus's prayer of thanksgiving, with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical marking.

FullText: » All things have been delivered to me from my Father . Only the Father knows the Son , and only the Son knows the Father . The Son tells others about the Father so that they can know him too .
ASTrns_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('have been delivered'), and the lexical verb 'deliver' licenses both an external argument (agent: 'my Father') and an internal argument (patient: 'All things'), satisfying the passive condition in set_to_1_if.
ASUncc_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('have been delivered'), and the lexical verb 'deliver' licenses an external argument (the Father, expressed in a by-phrase equivalent 'from my Father'), which disqualifies it from being unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'have been delivered' is passive voice with 'all things' functioning as an internal argument (theme/patient) rather than an external argument, licensing a direct object that receives the action.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'delivered' denotes a punctual boundary transition (completion of transfer) without a durative process phase, and the passive construction emphasizes the result state rather than a directed process toward a goal.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have been delivered' denotes a punctual transition into a new result state (things moving from not-delivered to delivered status), naturally permits time-point modification (e.g., 'were delivered at that moment'), and lacks measurable internal duration, representing the boundary point of transition itself.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have been delivered' denotes a completed transfer with an inherent endpoint and result state (things are now in a state of having been delivered), satisfying the set_to_0_if criterion of entailing a specific, defined result state.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have been delivered' entails a resulting change of state (things transitioning from not-delivered to delivered), which disqualifies it from AktSemel status.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'have been delivered' is morphologically marked with progressive/perfect aspect ('have been') indicating a completed dynamic event of transfer, which involves change-of-state and eventive unfolding incompatible with stative readings.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents a situation that has actually occurred in the actual world ('All things have been delivered to me'), with no indication of counterfactuality, contrary-to-fact condition, or unfulfilled wish.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have been delivered' is affirmative; no negator ('not', 'n't', 'never') or negative quantifier negates this eventuality.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have been delivered' entails a change of possession (things transferred from Father to subject) where that resulting state of possession holds at the reference time, matching the set_to_0_if criterion.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have been delivered' describes a change of possession (things transferred from Father to Son), and the resulting state of possession is currently held at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a completed transfer event (discrete bounded action) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, with no duration adverbials or habitual iteration present.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'have been delivered' is a passive form presenting a bounded transition event (delivery as a completed whole) with a construed endpoint, matching the set_to_1_if criterion for passive bounded transitions.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have been delivered' is in present perfect form asserting a realized fact in the actual world without modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical markers.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have been delivered' includes 'been' followed by a past participle in a progressive-like construction, which matches the set_to_1_if criterion for progressive perfect forms.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have been delivered' instantiates VcPass: it has the form auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle of transitive verb 'delivered', the grammatical subject 'All things' is the semantic patient (entity affected), and the agent is expressed in a 'by'-phrase ('by my Father').

FullText: » If you had known what this means , ‘ I desire mercy and not sacrifice , ’ ’ you would not have condemned the guiltless .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'had known' introduces an overt internal argument ('what this means'), satisfying the criterion for a transitive verb with a direct object.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'know' licenses an external argument (the subject 'you' as the knower/agent), making it unergative rather than unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'had known' licenses an internal argument (the clause 'what this means'), making it a transitive verb that does not instantiate ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'had known' denotes a punctual cognitive state or boundary transition (the moment of acquiring knowledge) without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint, fitting the set_to_0_if criterion for punctual change-of-state.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'had known' denotes a state of awareness that unfolds over measurable duration rather than a punctual transition into a new state; knowing is a durative mental state, not a boundary-crossing event like 'realize' or 'discover'.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had known' denotes a bounded cognitive state with a defined onset point (the moment of acquiring knowledge), lacks dynamic unfolding, and does not require sustained dynamic engagement, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for bounded perceptual/cognitive events marking the onset of awareness.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had known' entails a resulting change of state (transition from not knowing to knowing) and is not a single punctual occurrence without consequent result, thus it does not instantiate AktSemel.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'had known' denotes a mental state (knowledge) that would have held over time in the counterfactual conditional, with no dynamic change or eventive unfolding, matching the stative criterion for mental states.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The target 'had known' occurs in the protasis of a counterfactual conditional ('If you had known...') paired with the apodosis 'you would not have condemned', indicating a situation contrary to fact where the condition was not satisfied.
ClNegPol_explanation: The negation 'not' in the sentence applies to 'have condemned' (the consequent clause), not to the target gram 'had known' (the antecedent clause of the conditional), following the set_to_0_if rule that negation must negate the target gram itself.
DtExp_explanation: The gram 'had known' is a negative Perfect in a counterfactual conditional (if you had known), functioning as a claim about the absence of knowledge in the subject's history, which matches the set_to_1_if criterion for negative Perfects.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had known' appears in a counterfactual conditional clause ('If you had known...') that negates the actual occurrence of the event; no transition to a resulting state is entailed in reality.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a hypothetical conditional state focused on the transition into knowledge (learning/understanding what something means) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and lacks duration adverbials indicating extension to reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'had known' denotes a stative situation (knowledge/cognition) without a construed boundary or transition, and the clause does not present a bounded occurrence but rather a hypothetical condition with internal temporal span.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had known' appears in a counterfactual conditional construction ('If you had known...') that presents a hypothetical, non-realized situation contrary to fact.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary is morphologically past ('had' + past participle 'known'), which instantiates the past perfect form rather than the simple present perfect, meeting the first set_to_1_if criterion.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had known' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'you' as the semantic agent (the one doing the knowing), not a passive construction with 'have/had' + 'been' + past participle.

FullText: » If you had known what this means , ‘ I desire mercy and not sacrifice , ’ ’ you would not have condemned the guiltless .

AktState_explanation: The verb 'condemned' denotes a bounded dynamic event involving a change-of-state (transitioning from not-condemned to condemned), which is incompatible with AktState; the conditional perfect construction emphasizes the eventive nature of this action.

TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'would not have condemned' contains the modal auxiliary 'would' in a conditional construction ('If you had known...you would not have'), presenting the situation as hypothetical and counterfactual rather than realized.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'would not have condemned' includes the modal auxiliary 'would' preceding 'have', which matches the set_to_1_if criterion for modal perfect forms.

FullText: » Look ! My servant whom I have chosen . I am well pleased with him . I will put my Spirit upon him and he will declare judgment to the nations .

AktState_explanation: The verb 'have chosen' denotes a bounded change-of-state event (selection/decision) with a clear endpoint, involving dynamic volitional activity and internal eventive unfolding, which is incompatible with AktState.

TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'whom I have chosen' presents the selection of the servant as a single bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint, construing the choice as a completed transition event rather than an ongoing or habitual action.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have chosen' is in the present perfect tense asserting a realized fact about the servant in the actual world, with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical marking.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have chosen' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by the past participle 'chosen', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the simple present perfect form.

FullText: » But if I cast out demons by God’s Spirit , then the kingdom of God has come to you .

ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a change-of-location/appearance event where the sole argument 'the kingdom of God' functions as an internal argument (theme) undergoing a change of state rather than as an external argument initiating an action.

AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (arrival/presence of the kingdom of God), can be modified by specific time-point adverbials ('has come at that moment'), and represents a boundary crossing rather than an unfolding process.

AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has come' entails a transition into a new resulting state (the kingdom of God arriving/being present), which violates the AktSemel requirement of no resulting change of state.

ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has come' is not negated; it appears in a positive conditional clause asserting that the kingdom of God has arrived, with no negator ('not', 'n't', 'never') or negative quantifier modifying it.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has come' entails a change of location/state (the kingdom of God arriving/being present), and this resulting state holds at the reference time, matching the set_to_0_if criterion and the example 'The time has come.'
DtRes_explanation: The motion verb 'come' describes the kingdom of God arriving at a destination (to you), and the resulting state of presence/arrival persists at the reference time, indicating a change of state that currently holds.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a punctual transition (arrival of the kingdom) rather than a continuous state or activity extending to the reference time, and lacks duration adverbials or habitual iteration markers.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has come' is a transition event (arrival/movement into a state) construed as a completed whole, presenting the kingdom of God's arrival as a single bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint.

FullText: » Have you understood all these things ? « They said to him : » Yes . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'understood' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('you') and an overt direct object ('all these things'), satisfying the first condition for ASTrns=1.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'understand' licenses an external argument (the subject 'you' as the agent/experiencer) and takes a direct object ('all these things'), thus it does not meet the criteria for unaccusative verbs.

AktAch_explanation: The verb 'understood' denotes a punctual cognitive transition into awareness or recognition (matching CRI set_to_1_if criterion 4), representing the instantaneous moment of comprehension rather than an unfolding process of learning.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'understood' denotes a bounded perceptual/cognitive event marking the onset of comprehension, which is a single completed occurrence rather than a dynamic durative situation, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for bounded perceptual events.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have understood' entails a resulting change of state (transition from not understanding to understanding), which disqualifies it from AktSemel according to the set_to_0_if criteria.
AktState_explanation: The perfect aspect 'have understood' denotes a bounded cognitive event marking the completion of understanding (change from not understanding to understanding), which is a change-of-state event rather than a static mental state holding over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause is a direct question in the actual world ('Have you understood all these things?') with an affirmative response ('Yes'), presenting the situation as having actually occurred, not contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have understood' is in an interrogative clause with no negator (not, n't, never, or negative quantifier) directly negating it; the question is affirmative in form.
DtExp_explanation: The clause 'Have you understood all these things?' is a perception/comprehension verb focusing on whether the experience of understanding has occurred at least once in the subject's life history, rather than asserting a resulting state.
