FullText: » When the grain is ready he immediately sends men to cut it , because the time for cutting has come . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has come' is intransitive and does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); it only has an overt subject ('the time for cutting').
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes directed motion/change-of-location (arrival of a time/event) where the sole argument 'the time for cutting' undergoes a change without licensing an external argument, matching the unaccusative pattern.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes a change-of-location/appearance event where the sole argument (the time for cutting) functions as an internal argument (theme) undergoing a change of state rather than as an external argument initiating an action.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a punctual boundary transition (the arrival of a time point) without a durative process phase, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for punctual or near-instantaneous change-of-state.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (the arrival of the cutting time), naturally modifiable by time-point adverbials (e.g., 'has come at dawn'), and represents a definable threshold crossing without internal duration.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a bounded perceptual/cognitive event marking the onset of a specific state (the arrival of the cutting time), with an inherent endpoint and defined result state, not a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has come' entails a transition into a new resulting state (the time for cutting has arrived/is now present), which violates the AktSemel requirement of no resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a change-of-state event marking the onset or arrival of a temporal boundary (the time for cutting), which is a bounded perceptual/cognitive event involving dynamic change rather than a static condition.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'the time for cutting has come' presents a situation that has actually occurred in the actual world (the grain is ready, the time has arrived), with no indication of counterfactuality, unfulfilled wishes, or conditional reasoning.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has come' is not negated; it expresses a positive occurrence of the event (the time for cutting has arrived), and no negator applies to it.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has come' entails a change of state where a new temporal situation (the time for cutting) now holds at the reference time, matching the set_to_0_if criterion of introducing a current state of affairs.
DtRes_explanation: The motion verb 'come' describes a change of state where the time for cutting has arrived and that state (the time being present/at hand) currently holds at the reference time, satisfying the DtRes feature.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a transition into a new state (the arrival of the cutting time) rather than a situation holding continuously over a time span, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for verbs that entail transitions.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has come' is a transition event (arrival/occurrence of a time point) construed as a completed bounded whole, presenting the situation as a single occurrence with a construed endpoint rather than as ongoing or habitual.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has come' asserts the situation as a realized fact in the actual world (the time for cutting has actually arrived), with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has come' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'come', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has come' is active voice with 'come' as an intransitive verb where the grammatical subject 'the time' is the semantic agent/experiencer, not a patient; there is no passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle) and no by-phrase.

FullText: Their keepers ran off to the town to tell others what happened . People came to see what had taken place .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'take place' does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); it is an intransitive verb meaning 'occur' that requires no affected patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'take place' denotes an occurrence/event that happens without licensing an external argument; the sole argument 'what' functions as an internal argument (theme), matching the set_to_1_if criterion for existence, appearance, or occurrence without licensing an external argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'take place' licenses an internal argument (the event that occurred) and denotes a change-of-state/coming-into-being event where the subject functions as a theme rather than an external agent.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'take place' denotes a punctual or near-instantaneous event (an occurrence or happening) without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint, matching the set_to_0 criterion for boundary transitions.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'take place' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (an event occurring at a definable temporal boundary), naturally modifiable by time-point adverbials (e.g., 'at noon'), and lacks measurable internal duration, meeting multiple set_to_1 criteria.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had taken place' denotes a bounded, completed event with an inherent endpoint (the occurrence of an event), not a dynamic durative situation; it cannot be naturally modified by 'for X time' without semantic oddity, and it marks a single punctual occurrence rather than ongoing action.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had taken place' entails a transition into a resulting state (something happened that changed the situation), and the past perfect construction indicates a completed event with consequent results, not a punctual occurrence without change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'take place' denotes a bounded dynamic event (something happening/occurring) with an inherent endpoint, which involves eventive unfolding rather than a static condition holding over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'what had taken place' presents a situation that actually occurred in the narrative world (people came to see an event that did happen), using past perfect for temporal anteriority within the narrative, not expressing a counterfactual condition.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had taken place' is not negated; it appears in a positive relative clause describing what people came to see, with no negator directly modifying or negating the eventuality.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'had taken place' denotes a bounded event (something happening), but in context it describes a completed event that people came to witness/see, functioning as a past occurrence that motivated their action rather than as a life-history entry without resulting state implications.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'take place' is an activity/event verb describing an occurrence without entailing a resulting state that persists at the reference time; people came to see what happened (the event itself), not to observe a changed state currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (what happened at a specific prior moment) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span, and contains no duration adverbials or habitual markers.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'take place' is a transition event denoting an occurrence, and 'had taken place' presents it as a single bounded completed event in the narrative sequence, functioning as a discrete step in the discourse.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had taken place' is a past perfect form asserting a realized event in the actual world (what actually happened), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, making this a past perfect form rather than a simple present perfect, which satisfies the first set_to_1_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had taken place' is an active construction where 'take place' is an intransitive verb with 'had' as the auxiliary; there is no passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle), and the grammatical subject 'what' is the semantic agent/theme of the event, not a patient.

FullText: When they came to Jesus they saw the man who had been demon possessed . He was fully clothed and in full use of his senses . So they were afraid .
ASTrns_explanation: The passive construction 'had been demon possessed' licenses both an external argument (the demon/possessing agent) and an internal argument (the man as patient), satisfying the passive condition in set_to_1_if.
ASUncc_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('had been...possessed'), and the lexical verb 'possess' licenses an external argument (an agent/demon that possessed the man), making it unaccusative-incompatible.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'possessed' licenses an internal argument (the subject 'man' functions as the theme/patient being possessed by demons), making this a transitive construction that does not instantiate ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The past perfect 'had been demon possessed' describes a state or condition that the man was in, not a durative process directed toward a defined endpoint; it lacks the inherent goal-directedness and process-culmination structure required for AktAcc.
AktAch_explanation: The past perfect 'had been demon possessed' denotes a punctual transition into a state of possession (crossing a threshold from non-possessed to possessed), which is a change-of-state event that can be modified by time-point adverbials and does not permit interruption as an ongoing process.
AktAct_explanation: The situation denotes a bounded perceptual/cognitive state with a defined result state (possession ending), lacks dynamic unfolding in the present moment, and is treated as a single completed occurrence rather than an ongoing activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had been demon possessed' entails a resulting change of state (transition from possessed to no longer possessed), as evidenced by the contrast with the subsequent state 'He was fully clothed and in full use of his senses,' indicating a state change rather than a punctual non-resultative occurrence.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'had been demon possessed' is morphologically marked as progressive perfect aspect ('had been + past participle'), which entails dynamic unfolding and is explicitly incompatible with stative readings per the set_to_0_if criteria.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'who had been demon possessed' uses past perfect morphology to express temporal anteriority within a narrative (the possession occurred before the moment of observation), describing a situation that actually occurred in the actual world, without any counterfactual marking.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had been demon possessed' is not negated; it describes a realized past state without any negator ('not', 'n't', negative quantifier, or 'never') directly modifying it.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'had been possessed' entails a change of state (from possessed to no longer possessed), and the resulting state of being free from possession holds at the reference time, as evidenced by the subsequent clauses describing his current condition ('fully clothed and in full use of his senses').
DtRes_explanation: The verb describes a change of state (from possessed to no longer possessed), and the resulting state of being free from demon possession is currently in effect at the reference time, as evidenced by the subsequent description that 'He was fully clothed and in full use of his senses.'
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a past state (demon possession) that has ended by the reference time, focusing on the transition from being possessed to no longer being possessed, rather than a situation holding continuously to the present.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'had been demon possessed' uses the passive form 'have been' + past participle and asserts a bounded transition into and out of the state of possession, presenting the situation as a completed whole rather than an ongoing process.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had been demon possessed' is a past perfect construction asserting a realized state in the actual world (the man's prior condition of possession), not a modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical construction.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, and 'been' is followed by the participle 'possessed', making this a past perfect progressive form that instantiates TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The complement of 'been' is the adjectival phrase 'demon possessed' (a non-verbal predicate describing a state), not a verbal past participle functioning as passive; 'been' functions as a copula here, not a passive auxiliary.

FullText: Entering the boat , the man who had been possessed with demons asked that he might be allowed to travel with him .
ASTrns_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('had been possessed'), and the lexical verb 'possess' licenses both an external argument (agent/demons) and an internal argument (patient/the man), satisfying the passive condition in set_to_1_if.
ASUncc_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('had been possessed'), and the lexical verb 'possess' licenses an external argument (the demons as the implicit agent), thus failing the unaccusative criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'possessed' licenses an internal argument ('with demons'), making it transitive; the subject functions as a theme/patient undergoing a state rather than as an external agent initiating an activity.
AktAcc_explanation: The state 'had been possessed' describes a resultant condition without a durative process phase; possession is a state rather than a gradual change-of-state event with an inherent endpoint, and the past perfect indicates a prior state rather than a bounded accomplishment with measurable duration.
AktAch_explanation: The past perfect progressive 'had been possessed' denotes a state or condition that unfolded over measurable duration rather than a punctual transition into a new state, and the possession itself lacks the instantaneous threshold-crossing quality required for AktAch.
AktAct_explanation: The past perfect 'had been possessed' denotes a bounded state with a defined result (the man was in a state of possession) rather than a dynamic durative activity; possession is a stative condition, not an ongoing action requiring sustained engagement.
AktSemel_explanation: The past perfect 'had been possessed' entails a resulting change of state (transition from not-possessed to possessed condition) and describes a state rather than a punctual dynamic occurrence.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'had been possessed' is morphologically marked as progressive perfect aspect (past perfect progressive), which entails dynamic unfolding and is explicitly incompatible with stative readings per the set_to_0_if criteria.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'who had been possessed with demons' uses past perfect morphology to express temporal anteriority within a narrative (the possession occurred before the man entered the boat), describing a situation that actually occurred in the actual world, without any counterfactual marking.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had been possessed' is not negated; it describes a realized state of the man who possessed demons, with no negator ('not', 'n't', 'never') or negative quantifier directly negating this eventuality.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'possessed' entails a change of state (being under demonic possession), and the past perfect 'had been possessed' describes a resulting state that held at the reference time of the narrative, not merely an entry in the subject's life history.
DtRes_explanation: The past perfect 'had been possessed' describes a prior state that no longer holds at the reference time (the man is now entering the boat and asking to travel, indicating he is no longer possessed); the resulting state does not persist at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a prior state of possession that has ended by the reference time (the man is now entering the boat and asking to travel), focusing on the transition out of that state rather than a situation holding continuously to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'had been possessed with demons' presents a bounded transition state (the man entering a state of possession) as a completed whole prior to the narrative action, functioning as a discrete characterization in the narrative sequence.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had been possessed' asserts a realized past state as a fact about the man, not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary is morphologically past ('had') and the gram includes 'been' followed by a progressive participle ('possessed'), satisfying two conditions for TLTS=1: past perfect with progressive aspect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had been possessed' instantiates VcPass: it has the form auxiliary 'had' + 'been' + past participle 'possessed' of a transitive verb, the grammatical subject 'the man' is the semantic patient (entity affected by possession with demons), and passive morphology is overt.

FullText: He went away and told others in Decapolis what great things Jesus had done for him . They all marveled .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'had done' introduces an overt internal argument ('what great things') functioning as a direct object, satisfying the criterion for a transitive construction with both external argument (Jesus) and internal argument.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'had done' licenses an external argument (Jesus as agent/initiator) and introduces a direct object ('what great things'), satisfying the set_to_0_if criteria for agentive verbs with objects.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'had done' licenses an internal argument ('what great things') functioning as a direct object, thus failing the requirement for ASUnrg which demands no internal argument be licensed or realized.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'had done' denotes a durative process of performing great things that culminates in a defined result state (things accomplished), fitting the criterion of directed action toward a goal with an inherent endpoint.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'had done' denotes a durative accomplishment (the performance of great things) that unfolds over measurable duration rather than a punctual transition into a new state, and it can naturally occur as a complement of 'finish' (e.g., 'finish doing great things'), which indicates a process phase rather than an instantaneous boundary crossing.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had done' denotes a completed action with a specific result state (great things accomplished for him), which entails an inherent endpoint rather than a dynamic, durative, atelic situation.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had done' entails a resulting change of state (great things accomplished for him) and does not constitute a single punctual occurrence without consequent result.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'had done' denotes a bounded dynamic event (great things Jesus accomplished) involving internal eventive unfolding and change, which fails the stative criteria and matches the set_to_0_if condition for change-of-state and dynamic activity.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'Jesus had done for him' uses past perfect morphology to express temporal anteriority within a narrative (the healing occurred before he went away and told others), without any indication that the situation is contrary to fact; it describes what actually occurred.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had done' is not negated; it appears in a positive relative clause describing great things Jesus accomplished, with no negator directly modifying or negating the eventuality expressed by the target gram.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'had done' denotes actions (great things) with a resulting state of benefit ('for him'), focusing on the accomplished deeds and their effects rather than on the occurrence as a life-history entry.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had done' describes completed actions (great things Jesus performed) whose effects/results persist and are currently relevant at the reference time, as evidenced by the fact that others are being told about these accomplished deeds and marveling at them.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'what great things Jesus had done for him' describes discrete bounded events (miracles/actions performed) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and the focus is on the accomplishment of specific acts rather than duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The past perfect 'had done' presents Jesus's actions as a bounded, completed whole—a discrete set of miraculous events that the man witnessed and is now reporting as accomplished facts within the narrative sequence.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had done' is in a reported speech context asserting realized facts about Jesus's actions in the actual world, not presenting a hypothetical, conditional, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past tense, forming a past perfect construction ('had done'), which instantiates TLTS according to the first set_to_1_if criterion.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had done' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'Jesus' as the semantic agent (doer), not passive voice with a patient subject; there is no auxiliary 'been' and no passive morphology.

FullText: He looked around to see the woman who had done this thing .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'had done' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('who', referring to 'the woman') and an overt direct object ('this thing'), satisfying the first set_to_1 condition.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'done' licenses an external argument (the woman as agent) and introduces a direct object ('this thing'), satisfying the set_to_0_if criteria for verbs that license external arguments and contain overt objects.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'done' licenses an internal argument ('this thing' as direct object), so it does not instantiate ASUnrg which requires no internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'do' in 'had done this thing' is punctual and denotes a completed action without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint; it lacks the inherent process-toward-completion structure required for AktAcc.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'do' in 'had done this thing' denotes a durative activity or process that unfolds over measurable duration rather than a punctual transition into a new state, and lacks the instantaneous boundary-crossing quality required for AktAch.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had done this thing' denotes a bounded, completed action with an inherent endpoint (the completion of 'this thing'), not a dynamic durative situation without inherent result state.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had done this thing' entails a resulting change of state (something was done/accomplished), which violates the requirement for AktSemel that the event must not entail a resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'had done' denotes a bounded dynamic event (a completed action with internal development and an inherent endpoint), which fails the stative criteria and matches the set_to_0_if condition for change-of-state and eventive unfolding.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'who had done this thing' uses past perfect morphology to express temporal anteriority within a narrative (the action of doing preceded the looking around), describing an event that actually occurred in the story world, with no indication of counterfactuality.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had done' is not negated; the negation rule does not apply as there is no negator ('not', 'n't', negative quantifier, or 'never') directly negating the eventuality of 'had done'.
DtExp_explanation: The past perfect 'had done' presents the action as part of the woman's life history without asserting a resulting state; the focus is on the occurrence of the action itself rather than on any persistent consequence.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had done' describes a completed action with a resulting state (the thing has been done) that holds at the reference time of the narrative, meeting the criterion for a change of state that persists.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (the doing of 'this thing') rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and the focus is on the completed action itself, not on duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'had done' presents a completed transition event (the action of doing 'this thing') as a bounded whole, construed as a single accomplished occurrence prior to the main narrative event.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had done' is a past perfect form asserting a realized fact in the actual world (the woman performed the action), not a modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical construction.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, making this a past perfect form rather than a simple present perfect, which satisfies the first set_to_1_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had done' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'who' (the woman) as the semantic agent performing the action, not a passive construction with 'have/had' + 'been' + past participle.

FullText: He said : » Daughter your faith has made you whole . Go in peace and be healed of your affliction . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has made' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('your faith') and an overt direct object ('you'), instantiating the transitive pattern where the event is directed from the external argument toward and affecting the internal argument.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'made' licenses an external argument (your faith) that functions as an agent/initiator causing the change-of-state in the internal argument (you), and the clause contains an overt direct object (you), satisfying the set_to_0_if conditions.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has made whole' licenses an internal argument ('you'), making it transitive and thus not an unergative one-participant event.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has made whole' denotes a punctual or near-instantaneous change-of-state (transition from afflicted to healed) without a durative process phase, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for boundary transitions like 'arrive' or 'break'.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has made whole' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (wholeness/healing) that occurs at a definable threshold moment, naturally modifiable by time-point adverbials (e.g., 'has made you whole at that moment'), and lacks measurable internal duration.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has made whole' denotes a bounded, perfective event with an inherent endpoint (the state of being made whole) and a defined result state, fitting the set_to_0_if criteria for situations entailing specific endpoints.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has made whole' entails a transition into a new resulting state (from afflicted to healed), which violates the AktSemel requirement of no resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has made whole' denotes a change-of-state event (transformation from afflicted to healed) involving dynamic progression and an event boundary, which is incompatible with AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'your faith has made you whole' presents a situation that has actually occurred in the actual world (the healing is real and completed), with no indication of counterfactuality, contrary-to-fact condition, or unfulfilled wish.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has made whole' is not negated; the sentence expresses a positive realization of the event (faith has made you whole), with no negator applying to this verb.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has made whole' entails a change of state (from afflicted to healed) where the resulting state of wholeness/healing holds at the reference time, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for verbs that entail a change of state with a persisting result.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has made whole' describes a change of state (from afflicted to healed/whole), and the resulting state of being whole/healed is currently in effect at the reference time, as confirmed by the parallel imperative 'be healed of your affliction' indicating the state now holds.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'has made whole' denotes a transition into a new state (from afflicted to healed) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and there are no duration adverbials indicating continuous extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has made whole' is construed as a completed transition event (a change of state from afflicted to healed), presenting the situation as a single bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint rather than as ongoing or habitual.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has made whole' is asserted as a realized fact in the actual world within direct speech, with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical markers present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has made whole' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by past participle 'made', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the simple present perfect pattern.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'your faith' is the semantic agent (doer) and 'you' is the semantic patient (object), making this active voice, not passive voice; there is no auxiliary 'been' and no passive morphology.

FullText: John the Baptist had become well known . King Herod heard that John had risen from the dead . Therefore he was doing these powerful works .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'become' functions as a copula linking the subject to a predicative complement ('well known'), rather than introducing an internal argument, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for copular verbs.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'become' denotes a change-of-state event where the sole argument 'John the Baptist' functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) undergoing a transition to a known state, with no external argument licensed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'become' licenses an internal argument (the predicative adjective 'well known' functioning as a complement), and the sole argument 'John' functions as a theme/patient undergoing a change-of-state rather than as an external agent, matching the unaccusative pattern in set_to_0_if.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'become' denotes a punctual boundary transition from unknown to known state without a durative process phase, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for punctual change-of-state.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'become well known' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (from unknown to known) that can be modified by specific time-point adverbials and lacks measurable internal duration, satisfying the criteria for AktAch.
AktAct_explanation: The verb phrase 'had become well known' denotes a change of state with an inherent endpoint (the transition to being well known), lacking the dynamic durative unfolding characteristic of activities.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had become well known' entails a transition into a resulting state (the state of being well known), which violates the requirement that AktSemel events must not entail a resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb phrase 'had become well known' denotes a change-of-state event (transition from unknown to known) with an internal eventive boundary marking the onset of the state, which is incompatible with AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'had become well known' presents a factual past event using past perfect morphology to express temporal anteriority within a narrative, without any indication that the situation is contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had become well known' is not negated; it expresses a positive state that John the Baptist had achieved fame, with no negator ('not', 'n't', 'never', or negative quantifier) directly negating this eventuality.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'become well known' entails a change of state (from unknown to well known) where that resulting state holds at the reference time, matching the set_to_0_if criterion.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'become' describes a change of state from unknown to well-known, and the resulting state (being well-known) holds at the reference time indicated by the past perfect construction in narrative context.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'had become well known' denotes a transition into a new state (becoming known) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and there is no duration adverbial or 'since' clause indicating continuous duration to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'become' is a transition event, and 'had become well known' presents this transition as a single completed bounded occurrence in the narrative sequence, meeting the criterion for perfective aspect.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had become well known' is a past perfect form asserting a realized fact in the actual world (John the Baptist's fame was an established reality), with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical markers present.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, making this a past perfect form rather than a simple present perfect, which satisfies the set_to_1_if condition for past auxiliary marking.
VcPass_explanation: The complement of 'become' is the adjective phrase 'well known' functioning as a predicate adjective, not a past participle in passive voice; 'become' is a copular verb, not an auxiliary marking passive voice.

FullText: John the Baptist had become well known . King Herod heard that John had risen from the dead . Therefore he was doing these powerful works .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'risen' is intransitive and does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); it only takes a prepositional phrase 'from the dead' which is not a grammatical object.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'risen' denotes directed motion/change-of-location (from dead to alive/resurrected) where the sole argument 'he' (John) functions as an internal argument undergoing displacement, with no external argument licensed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'risen' denotes a change-of-location event where the sole argument (John) functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) undergoing a change of state, not as an external argument/agent initiating an activity.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'risen from the dead' denotes a punctual boundary transition (crossing from dead to alive state) without a durative process phase, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for punctual change-of-state.
AktAch_explanation: The verb phrase 'had risen from the dead' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (from dead to alive), lacks measurable internal duration, and naturally accepts time-point modification (e.g., 'had risen at that moment'), meeting the criteria for AktAch.
AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'had risen from the dead' denotes a bounded perceptual/cognitive event with a specific, inherent endpoint (the completion of rising), marking the onset of awareness of a completed occurrence, not a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had risen from the dead' entails a transition into a new resulting state (being alive after being dead), which violates the AktSemel requirement of no resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb phrase 'had risen from the dead' denotes a bounded change-of-state event (transition from dead to alive) with a clear endpoint, involving dynamic eventive unfolding incompatible with stative aspect.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'had risen from the dead' expresses what King Herod heard (a reported belief), not a counterfactual condition; it uses past perfect for temporal anteriority within narrative discourse without marking the situation as contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had risen from the dead' is not negated; the negation in the sentence ('heard that John had risen') applies to the matrix verb 'heard' in Herod's perception, not to the target gram itself, which reports what Herod believed to be true.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'risen from the dead' entails a change of state (from dead to alive) where that resulting state holds at the reference time, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for verbs entailing a change of state that persists.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'risen from the dead' describes a change of state (from dead to alive) and the context indicates this resulting state holds at the reference time when Herod hears the news.
DtUniv_explanation: The predicate 'had risen from the dead' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (from dead to alive) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and contains no duration adverbials indicating extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'risen from the dead' is a transition event (change of state from dead to alive) construed as a completed whole, and the clause functions as a discrete bounded event in the narrative sequence.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had risen from the dead' is asserted as a realized fact within the narrative world (what King Herod heard/believed), not presented as hypothetical, conditional, or uncertain through modal auxiliaries or subjunctive constructions.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, making this a past perfect form rather than a simple present perfect, which satisfies the set_to_1_if condition for past auxiliary marking.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had risen from the dead' is an active construction where the grammatical subject 'John' is the semantic agent (the one who rose), not the patient; there is no passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle), and no by-phrase expressing an external agent.

FullText: The apostles gathered together with Jesus . They told him all things they had done and taught .
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'had done' introduces an overt internal argument ('all things'), satisfying the condition for an overt direct object in a transitive construction.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'do' licenses an external argument (the apostles as agent/initiator performing actions), which violates the unaccusative requirement that no external argument be licensed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'had done' licenses an internal argument ('all things'), making it transitive and thus not an unergative one-participant event.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'had done' denotes a punctual or completed action without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint; it describes a past accomplishment rather than an event with inherent process and culmination structure.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'had done' denotes a durative activity or process (the apostles' various actions and teachings) that unfolded over measurable duration rather than a punctual transition into a new state, and it naturally permits completion ('finish doing'), failing all criteria for AktAch.
AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'had done' denotes a completed action with an inherent endpoint (the apostles finished doing things and are now reporting them), lacking the dynamic, durative, homogeneous quality required for AktAct classification.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had done' entails a transition into a resulting state (completion of actions) and unfolds over measurable duration rather than occurring as a single punctual occurrence.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'had done' denotes a bounded dynamic event with internal eventive unfolding and change, involving active progression and completion, which is incompatible with stative readings.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'they had done' expresses temporal anteriority within a narrative (the apostles' past actions before reporting to Jesus), not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had done' is not negated; it appears in a positive declarative context where the apostles affirmatively 'had done' things they reported to Jesus.
DtExp_explanation: The gram 'had done' presents the apostles' actions as part of their life history without asserting a resulting state; it answers whether they performed these actions, focusing on the occurrence of the experience rather than on any persisting consequence.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had done' describes past activities without indicating a resulting state that persists at the reference time; it is a narrative report of completed actions rather than a transition to a state currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'they had done and taught' describes discrete bounded events (things accomplished) that the apostles reported to Jesus, rather than a continuous situation holding over a time span, and lacks duration adverbials or habitual markers.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The past perfect 'had done' presents the apostles' actions as bounded, completed events that occurred prior to the narrative moment when they reported to Jesus, construing them as discrete accomplishments rather than ongoing or habitual situations.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had done' asserts a realized past action (the apostles' completed activities) as a fact in the narrative, with no modal, conditional, counterfactual, or hypothetical marking.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, making this a past perfect form rather than a simple present perfect, which satisfies the first set_to_1_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had done' is active voice where the grammatical subject 'they' (the apostles) is the semantic agent performing the action, not a patient; there is no passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle) and no by-phrase.

FullText: They had not understood the loaves of bread . Their minds were closed .
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'had not understood' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('They') and an overt direct object ('the loaves of bread'), satisfying the first condition for ASTrns=1.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'understand' licenses an external argument (the subject 'They' as agent/experiencer) and takes a direct object ('the loaves of bread'), so it does not meet the criteria for unaccusative verbs.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'understood' licenses an internal argument ('the loaves of bread' as direct object), so it does not instantiate ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'understand' denotes a punctual cognitive state-change without a durative process phase; it lacks an inherent endpoint or result state that culminates a directed process, and cannot naturally be modified by 'in X time' adverbials indicating bounded completion.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'understand' denotes a punctual cognitive transition into awareness or recognition (matching CRI set_to_1_if criterion 4), where the moment of comprehension represents crossing a definable threshold from non-understanding to understanding, lacking measurable internal duration.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'understand' denotes a bounded cognitive event marking the onset of awareness or comprehension, which is a single completed occurrence rather than a dynamic durative situation with sustained engagement.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'understand' entails a transition into a resulting state of comprehension, which violates the requirement that AktSemel events must not entail a resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'understand' denotes a bounded cognitive event marking the onset of awareness or comprehension, which is a change-of-state from not understanding to understanding, thus encoding eventive change rather than a static mental state condition.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents a factual past situation ('They had not understood') as having actually occurred in the narrative world, using past perfect for temporal anteriority without any counterfactual marking, conditional structure, or indication that the situation is contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is directly negated with 'not' in 'had not understood', which matches the first set_to_1_if condition and instantiates ClNegPol.
DtExp_explanation: This is a negative Perfect ('had not understood') which functions as a claim about the absence of the event in the subject's life history, matching the set_to_1_if criterion.
DtRes_explanation: The negated clause 'had not understood' does not entail a resulting state; negation blocks the transition to a state of understanding, so no change of state holds at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a state of non-understanding (a transition into a state of not comprehending) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, with no duration adverbials or iterative framing indicating extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause is negated with 'not' and presents a stative situation (understanding as a mental state) without a construed boundary or transition, rather than asserting a bounded completed event.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had not understood' is a past perfect form asserting a realized fact about their mental state in the narrative, with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical marking.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past tense, making this a past perfect form rather than a simple present perfect, which satisfies the set_to_1_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'They' is the semantic agent (doer) and 'the loaves of bread' is the semantic patient (object), making this active voice, not passive voice.

FullText: Then he replied : » Thanks to your answer you may leave for the demon has gone out of your daughter . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has gone out of' introduces an overt external argument ('demon') and an overt internal argument ('your daughter'), satisfying the transitive pattern where the event is directed from the agent toward an affected patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'has gone out of' denotes a change-of-location/directed motion event where the sole argument 'the demon' functions as an internal argument (theme) undergoing displacement, with no external argument licensed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has gone out of' licenses an internal argument ('your daughter' as the source/location from which the demon departs), making it a transitive construction that does not meet the ASUnrg criteria of a one-participant event with only an external argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'has gone out of' denotes a directed motion/change-of-state toward a defined goal (the demon exiting the daughter), involving a durative process that culminates in a result state (the demon no longer being inside), matching the AktAcc feature.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has gone out of' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (the demon's departure from the daughter), can be modified by specific time-point adverbials ('at that moment'), and represents a definable threshold crossing rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb phrase 'has gone out of' denotes a completed action with an inherent endpoint (the demon's departure from the daughter), resulting in a defined result state, which satisfies the set_to_0_if criterion for bounded events with specific endpoints.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has gone out of' entails a resulting change of state (the demon transitions from being inside to being outside the daughter), which disqualifies it from AktSemel.
AktState_explanation: The verb phrase 'has gone out of' denotes a change-of-state event (the demon departing from the daughter) involving dynamic progression and an event boundary, which is incompatible with AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'the demon has gone out of your daughter' presents a situation as having actually occurred in the actual world (the demon has departed), not as contrary to fact, and contains no counterfactual marking.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has gone out of' is not negated; the sentence affirms that the demon has departed from the daughter, with no negator ('not', 'n't', 'never') or negative quantifier modifying this eventuality.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has gone out of' entails a change of state (the demon's departure from the daughter) where the resulting state—that the demon is no longer in/possessing the daughter—holds at the reference time, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for verbs entailing change of state.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has gone out of' describes a change of state (the demon's departure from the daughter) where the resulting state (demon no longer present in/possessing the daughter) persists at the reference time, matching the DtRes feature.
DtUniv_explanation: The predicate 'has gone out of' denotes a punctual transition (the demon leaving the daughter) rather than a continuous state or activity extending to the reference time, matching the negative example pattern of transition-focused verbs.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has gone out of' is a transition event (departure/exit) construed as a completed whole, presenting a bounded occurrence with a clear endpoint rather than an ongoing or stative situation.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has gone out of' asserts the demon's departure as a realized fact in the actual world within the narrative, not as projected, hypothetical, or uncertain.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has gone out of' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by past participle 'gone', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the simple present perfect form that sets the feature to 0.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has gone out of' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'demon' as the semantic agent (the entity performing the action of leaving), not a passive construction with auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle.

FullText: They came down the mountain . He warned them not to tell anyone what they had seen until after the Son of man had risen from the dead .
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'had seen' introduces an overt grammatical subject 'they' and an overt direct object 'what', satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition for a transitive construction with both subject and object.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'see' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'they' who are doing the seeing) and takes an internal object ('what'), making it transitive and not unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'had seen' licenses an internal argument ('what'), making it transitive and thus not an unergative one-participant event.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'had seen' denotes a punctual perceptual event (the moment of perception) without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint, and cannot be naturally modified by 'in X time' to indicate bounded completion.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'had seen' denotes a punctual perceptual transition into awareness/recognition of a visual event, which is a near-instantaneous crossing of the threshold from not-seeing to seeing, meeting the criteria for AktAch.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had seen' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness (seeing something), which is a single completed occurrence rather than a dynamic durative situation, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for perceptual events.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had seen' entails a resulting change of state (transition from not having seen to having seen something), which disqualifies it from AktSemel status.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'had seen' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the completion of visual awareness, which encodes eventive change rather than a static condition, triggering the set_to_0_if criterion for bounded perceptual events.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'what they had seen' uses past perfect to express temporal anteriority within a narrative (the seeing occurred before the warning), not to indicate a counterfactual situation; the event actually occurred in the story world.
ClNegPol_explanation: The negation 'not' applies to the embedded infinitive 'to tell', not to the target gram 'had seen'; the negation does not negate the eventuality of seeing.
DtExp_explanation: The negative Perfect 'had not seen' (implicit in the context of what they witnessed) functions as a claim about the occurrence of an event in the subject's life history, focusing on the experience of having witnessed something rather than on a resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had seen' describes a perception event (seeing) that does not entail a resulting state persisting at the reference time; it is a past experiential event with no change of state currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'what they had seen' describes a discrete bounded event (the act of witnessing something) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and contains no duration adverbials or habitual markers indicating continuous extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'what they had seen' presents the act of seeing as a completed, bounded event prior to the reference time (the warning), construed as a discrete occurrence rather than an ongoing or habitual situation.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had seen' is a past perfect form asserting a realized event in the actual world (what they had witnessed on the mountain), not a modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical construction.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, making this a past perfect form rather than a simple present perfect, which satisfies the first set_to_1_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had seen' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'they' as the semantic agent (doer) and the semantic patient 'what' as the grammatical object, not passive voice.

FullText: They came down the mountain . He warned them not to tell anyone what they had seen until after the Son of man had risen from the dead .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'risen' is intransitive with no overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); the prepositional phrase 'from the dead' is an adjunct indicating source/origin, not an affected patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'risen' denotes directed motion/change-of-location (rising from the dead) where the sole argument (Son of man) functions as an internal argument undergoing displacement, with no external argument licensed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'risen' denotes a change-of-location event where the sole argument (Son of man) functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) undergoing a change of state, not as an external argument initiating an action.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'had risen from the dead' denotes a punctual boundary transition (crossing from dead to alive state) without a durative process phase, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for punctual change-of-state events.
AktAch_explanation: The verb phrase 'had risen from the dead' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (from dead to alive), lacks measurable internal duration, and naturally accepts time-point modification (e.g., 'had risen at that moment'), meeting multiple set_to_1 criteria.
AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'had risen from the dead' denotes a specific, inherent endpoint (the completion of rising/resurrection) and a defined result state (being alive after death), satisfying the set_to_0_if criteria for bounded events with inherent endpoints.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had risen from the dead' entails a transition into a new resulting state (from dead to alive), which violates the AktSemel requirement that the event must not entail a resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb phrase 'had risen from the dead' denotes a change-of-state event (transition from dead to alive) with a clear boundary and dynamic progression, which violates the stative requirement of lacking internal eventive unfolding.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'had risen from the dead' expresses temporal anteriority within a narrative sequence (the Son of man rising after the warning is given), not a counterfactual condition contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The negation 'not' in the sentence applies to 'tell', not to the target gram 'had risen from the dead', which expresses a future positive eventuality in a temporal clause.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'rise from the dead' entails a change of state (from dead to alive) where the resulting state persists at the reference time, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for verbs entailing a change of state that holds at reference time.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'rise from the dead' describes a change of state (from dead to alive) where the resulting state (being alive/risen) is entailed to hold at the reference time specified in the conditional temporal clause.
DtUniv_explanation: The predicate 'had risen from the dead' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (moving from dead to alive) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and contains no duration adverbials indicating extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'had risen from the dead' is a transition event (rise = change of state/location) construed as a completed whole, presenting a bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint rather than an ongoing or habitual situation.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had risen from the dead' appears in a temporal clause ('until after the Son of man had risen from the dead') that expresses a future/projected event relative to the warning, presenting it as hypothetical and not yet realized at the time of speaking.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, making this a past perfect form rather than a simple present perfect, which satisfies the set_to_1_if condition for past auxiliary marking.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had risen from the dead' is active voice (past perfect of the intransitive verb 'rise'), not passive voice; there is no auxiliary 'been' + past participle structure, and the grammatical subject 'Son of man' is the semantic agent performing the action.

FullText: » But I say to you that Elijah has come . They have done to him what ever they wished just as it is written about him . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has come' is intransitive and does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object), matching the set_to_0_if criterion for verbs without overt internal arguments.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes directed motion/change-of-location where the sole argument (Elijah) undergoes displacement as an internal argument, and no external argument is licensed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes a change-of-location event where the sole argument (Elijah) functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) undergoing movement rather than as an external argument initiating an action.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a punctual boundary transition (arrival at a location/state) without a durative process phase, matching the negative criterion for near-instantaneous change-of-state events like 'arrive'.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (arrival at a location or in time) that can be modified by specific time-point adverbials (e.g., 'has come at that moment') and lacks measurable internal duration, representing a crossing of a definable threshold.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a punctual event with an inherent endpoint (arrival at a location/state), not a dynamic durative activity; it cannot be naturally modified by 'for X time' without semantic oddity, and represents a single completed occurrence rather than an ongoing situation.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has come' entails a transition into a resulting state (Elijah's arrival and presence), which violates the requirement that AktSemel events must not entail a resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a bounded perceptual/cognitive event marking the onset of arrival (change-of-state from not-arrived to arrived), which is a dynamic eventive boundary incompatible with stative readings.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'Elijah has come' presents the situation as having actually occurred in the actual world without any counterfactual marking, open conditional structure, or indication of unfulfilled wishes.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has come' is not negated; it appears in a positive assertion ('Elijah has come') with no negator directly modifying it or its auxiliary.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has come' entails a change of location (Elijah arriving), and the resulting state of his presence holds at the reference time, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for transitions into new physical locations where the subject remains at that location.
DtRes_explanation: The motion verb 'come' indicates arrival, but the context ('They have done to him what ever they wished') suggests the mover has not remained at the destination—Elijah has come and then been acted upon, implying he is no longer in the state of having just arrived, similar to the 'been to Rome' experiential example.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a punctual transition event (arrival) rather than a continuous state or activity extending to the reference time, matching the negative example pattern of transition-focused verbs.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'come' is a transition event construed as a completed whole, and the clause presents a single bounded occurrence (Elijah's arrival) as a discrete fact in the narrative.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has come' is a present perfect that asserts Elijah's arrival as a realized fact in the actual world, with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical marking.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has come' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'come', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The form 'has come' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'Elijah' as the semantic agent (the one who came), not a passive construction with auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle.

FullText: » But I say to you that Elijah has come . They have done to him what ever they wished just as it is written about him . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have done' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('They') and an overt direct object ('to him what ever they wished'), satisfying the transitive pattern with both external and internal arguments.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'have done' licenses an external argument (the subject 'They' as agent) and introduces a direct object ('to him'), satisfying the set_to_0_if criteria for agentive verbs with objects.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'have done' licenses an internal argument ('to him what ever they wished'), making it transitive and thus not an unergative one-participant event.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'have done' denotes a punctual or near-instantaneous completed action without a durative process phase; it describes what 'they' did to Elijah as a discrete event rather than a gradual process directed toward a defined endpoint.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have done' denotes a general action that unfolds over measurable duration (treating him in various ways) rather than a punctual transition into a new state, and it naturally permits the durative framing 'they finished doing to him what they wished,' indicating a process phase rather than a boundary-crossing achievement.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have done' denotes a bounded, completed action with an inherent endpoint (they performed certain actions to him), not a dynamic durative situation without inherent completion.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have done to him what ever they wished' entails a resulting change of state (harm or mistreatment inflicted upon Elijah), which disqualifies it from AktSemel status.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have done' denotes a bounded dynamic event (the action of doing something to someone) involving internal eventive unfolding and change, which is incompatible with the stative feature AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'They have done to him what ever they wished' presents a situation that actually occurred in the actual world (Elijah has come and they did treat him as they wished), with no indication of counterfactuality, contrary-to-fact condition, or unfulfilled wish.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have done' is not negated; it is a positive assertion that they performed actions to Elijah, with no negator ('not', 'n't', 'never') or negative quantifier modifying it.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have done' (referring to actions performed on Elijah) entails a change of state or condition affecting the subject, and that resulting state persists at the reference time, making it incompatible with the DtExp feature which requires focus on life-history occurrence rather than resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have done' describes a completed action (treating Elijah) without entailing a resulting state that persists at the reference time; it is a bare activity/event in the past with no current state effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'They have done to him what ever they wished' describes discrete bounded events (specific actions performed on Elijah) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for 'one or more discrete bounded events.'
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'They have done to him what ever they wished' presents a completed action as a bounded whole event in a narrative sequence, construing the doing as a single accomplished occurrence rather than an ongoing or habitual situation.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have done' is in present perfect form asserting a realized action in the actual world ('They have done to him what ever they wished'), with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical marking.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have done' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by past participle 'done' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the simple present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have done' is active voice with grammatical subject 'They' as the semantic agent (doer) and 'to him' as the affected entity; it lacks the passive auxiliary structure 'have been + past participle' required for VcPass.

FullText: Jesus asked his father : » How long has this been going on ? « He said : » Since he was a child . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has been going on' is a passive progressive form of an intransitive verb that does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); it describes a state or activity without an affected patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'going on' licenses an external argument (the implicit subject 'this' referring to an event/situation that is the agent/initiator of the ongoing state), and the clause is not a change-of-state or directed motion event without an external argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'going on' licenses an implicit internal argument (the situation/event that has been occurring), making it transitive rather than a one-participant unergative verb with only an external argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'has been going on' is dynamic and durative but lacks an inherent endpoint or result state; it naturally takes 'for X time' (since he was a child) rather than 'in X time', indicating an ongoing situation without defined completion.
AktAch_explanation: The verb phrase 'has been going on' denotes a durative process unfolding over measurable duration (since childhood) rather than a punctual transition into a new state, and naturally permits the completion frame 'finish going on', disqualifying it from AktAch.
AktAct_explanation: The verb phrase 'has been going on' denotes a dynamic, durative situation that unfolds over time without an inherent endpoint, naturally accepts 'for X time' modification (as evidenced by the context 'How long has this been going on'), and is homogeneous—any subinterval of the ongoing situation instantiates the same type of event.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has been going on' unfolds over a measurable duration (since childhood) rather than occurring as a single punctual occurrence, violating the requirement for instantaneous, bounded events.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'has been going on' is morphologically marked as progressive aspect (present perfect progressive), which according to CRI set_to_0_if entails dynamic unfolding and is incompatible with stative readings.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'has been going on' presents a situation that has actually occurred in the actual world (a state that began in the past and continues to the present), with no indication of counterfactuality, contrary-to-fact conditions, or unfulfilled wishes.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has been going on' is not negated; the negator 'not' does not appear in the sentence, and no negative quantifiers or 'never' modify it.
DtExp_explanation: The clause contains the duration adverbial 'How long' and the response 'Since he was a child', indicating a continuing situation rather than a completed life experience, which triggers the set_to_0_if condition.
DtRes_explanation: The clause contains a duration adverbial ('How long') and the context ('Since he was a child') indicates a continuing situation rather than a completed transition to a resulting state, making this an experiential or durative aspect rather than a resultative one.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'has been going on' with the duration question 'How long' and the response 'Since he was a child' indicates a situation that has held continuously from a past starting point to the reference time, matching the set_to_1_if criteria for 'since' and duration adverbials.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'has been going on' contains the progressive form 'be' + '-ing' (going), which profiles the internal temporal development of the situation rather than presenting it as a bounded whole, and the duration adverbial 'How long' makes the internal temporal span accessible rather than packaging the situation as bounded.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has been going on' is present perfect aspect asserting a realized situation in the actual world (a state that began in the past and continues to the present), with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical marking.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has been going on' includes 'been' followed by a progressive participle 'going', which matches the set_to_1_if criterion for progressive perfect forms.
VcPass_explanation: The complement of 'been' is the present participle 'going' (not a past participle), making this an active progressive construction with copular 'been', not a passive voice construction.

FullText: They said nothing because they had had an argument between themselves about who was the greatest .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'had' (with 'argument' as overt direct object) introduces both an overt grammatical subject ('they') and an overt direct object ('an argument'), satisfying the first set_to_1 condition for ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'have' licenses an external argument (the subject 'they') and introduces a direct object ('an argument'), so it does not meet the criteria for unaccusative verbs.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'had' licenses an internal argument ('an argument'), making it transitive and thus not an unergative one-participant event.
AktAcc_explanation: An argument is a punctual or near-instantaneous interpersonal event that lacks a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint; it denotes a boundary transition (the argument occurred) rather than a gradual change-of-state culminating in a result condition.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'had an argument' unfolds over measurable duration as a process of disagreement rather than occurring at a single temporal boundary, and naturally permits the durative framing 'finish having an argument'.

FullText: » That which God has joined together let not man take apart separate . «

AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has joined together' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (the state of being joined), can be modified by specific time-point adverbials ('at that moment'), does not permit interruption ('one cannot meaningfully stop joining'), and lacks measurable internal duration, representing the boundary point of transition itself.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has joined together' denotes a completed action with an inherent endpoint (the state of being joined), not a dynamic durative situation; it cannot naturally be modified by 'for X time' without implying incompleteness, and it entails a specific result state rather than ongoing activity.

FullText: » Teacher , « he said : » I have observed all of these from my youth . «

TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have observed' is in present perfect tense asserting a realized fact about the speaker's past experience, with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical marking.

TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have left' is a present perfect asserting a realized action in the actual world (Peter and others have completed the action of leaving and following), with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical marking.

DtExp_explanation: The clause contains the explicit frequency adverb 'ever' indicating a claim about the absence of the event in the subject's life history, which is a negative Perfect that functions as a life-history entry rather than asserting a resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'sat on' describes a past action/experience without entailing a resulting state that persists at reference time; the colt's current state is not that someone is sitting on it, and the negation 'No man has ever sat on' further indicates no transition to a resulting state.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'No man has ever sat on that colt' describes a discrete bounded event (the act of sitting) that has not occurred, rather than a continuous state or habitual series extending to the reference time; the adverbial 'ever' emphasizes the absence of any occurrence, not duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'No man has ever sat on that colt' denotes a stative situation (the state of having never been sat upon) without a construed boundary or transition, and the negation with 'ever' profiles the unbounded absence of the event rather than a bounded completion.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has sat on' asserts a realized fact in the actual world (no man has ever sat on the colt), with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has sat on' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'sat', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the simple present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has sat on' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'No man' as the semantic agent (the doer), not passive voice with a patient subject.

FullText: When he taught , he said : » Is it not written , my house shall be called a house of prayer for all the nations ? But you have made it a den of robbers . «
Target: have made (it a den of robbers)
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have made' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('you') and an overt direct object ('it'), satisfying the first condition for ASTrns=1.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'have made' licenses an external argument (the agent 'you') and introduces a direct object ('it'), satisfying the set_to_0_if condition that the clause contains an overt direct object introduced by the target verb.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'made' licenses an internal argument ('it') and a predicative complement ('a den of robbers'), making it transitive and thus not an unergative one-participant event.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'have made it a den of robbers' denotes a durative process of transformation directed toward a defined result state (changing the house from a house of prayer into a den of robbers), which satisfies the criterion of gradual change-of-state culminating in a defined result condition.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have made' denotes a durative process of transformation (turning something into a den of robbers) that unfolds over measurable duration rather than a punctual transition at a temporal boundary, and it can naturally occur as a complement of 'finish' (e.g., 'you finished making it a den of robbers').
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have made it a den of robbers' entails a specific result state (transforming the house into a den of robbers) and denotes a bounded, telic event with an inherent endpoint, not a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have made it a den of robbers' entails a resulting change of state (the house transitioning from a house of prayer to a den of robbers), which disqualifies it from AktSemel.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have made' denotes a change-of-state event (transforming the house from a house of prayer into a den of robbers), involving dynamic eventive unfolding and an internal development from one state to another, which is incompatible with AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'you have made it a den of robbers' presents an actual situation that has occurred in the world (a factual accusation of what the addressees have actually done), not a counterfactual condition or unfulfilled wish.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have made' is not negated; the negation 'not' in the sentence applies to 'Is it not written' (a separate clause), not to the target gram.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have made' entails a change of state (the house's transformation from a house of prayer into a den of robbers), and that resulting state holds at the reference time, triggering the set_to_0_if condition.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have made' describes a change of state where the house has transitioned from being a house of prayer to being a den of robbers, and this resulting state (the house currently being a den of robbers) holds at the reference time of the utterance.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (the action of making the house into a den of robbers) resulting in a new state, focusing on the transition rather than a situation holding continuously over time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'you have made it a den of robbers' presents a completed transition event (the action of making/transforming the house into a den of robbers) as a single bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint, fitting the perfective aspect.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have made' asserts a realized action in the actual world as a factual accusation, not as projected, hypothetical, or non-actual.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have made' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by past participle 'made' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the simple present perfect form.
